refs.note: The figure of the Roman jurors averting their eyes from Q. Metellus Numidicus's account-books recurs as the model of senatorial probity in Cicero's middle-period courtroom speeches.

The argument from rewards as the engine of military service to the empire is the same argument deployed at De Provinciis Consularibus 29-32, a few weeks earlier. Both speeches are the senatorial-court doublet of the post-Luca position.

The lex Julia of 90 BC and Heraclea / Naples back at section 21 prepare the ground for the Calliphana-of-Velia precedent at 55: in both cases citizenship can be conferred by the Roman state on a federate.

The famous post-Luca self-reckoning at Pro Balbo 61 ("some things we wished, contended for, made trial of: they were not won") is the same political move as the De Provinciis Consularibus 25-26 confession of having been brought back into agreement with Caesar by the Senate.

Cicero recalls his own motions of 57-56 BC: the fifteen-day Caesarian supplicatio (proposed at De Provinciis 26-27), the army-pay vote, the ten legates, and the de-provinciis vote against succession --- as a single political record of senatorial accommodation with the proconsul.
